10.1016/j.jmp.2011.03.001
The hazard function  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       for a continuous variable  
                         x 
                       is defined as  
                         
                            (1) 
                            
                               λ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                     − 
                                     F 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               , 
                            
                         
                       where  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       are respectively the probability density and the cumulative probability. The hazard function is typically considered as the instantaneous risk of a state change at  
                         x 
                      , condition by the fact that the state change did not occur for lesser values of  
                         x 
                      . The concept of a hazard function initially arose in actuarial research to deal with state changes such as death or product failure ( Steffensen, 1930 ). In the product reliability context, hazard corresponds to the proportion of the surviving items that fail at  
                         x 
                      , and the state change is the transition from a good product to a product failure. Because many problems can be regarded as a state change, hazard functions have been used in many disciplines ( Bain, 1978; Klein & Moeschberger, 1997 ). Hazard functions have also been widely used in psychology.  Townsend and Ashby (1978, 1983)  introduced hazard functions in psychology as a tool for understanding the dynamics of information processing. For this application, the critical event for the hazard function is the completion of a task or process. A number of studies have used hazard functions in this context (e.g.  Ashby, Tein, & Balakrishnan, 1993; Balakrishnan, 1994; Bloxom, 1984; Burbeck & Luce, 1982; Colonius, 1988; Maddox, Ashby, & Gottlob, 1998; Thomas, 1971; Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). In other applications, the critical event for the hazard function is the loss of a memory trace (e.g.  Chechile, 1987, 2006; Wickens, 1998, 1999 ). Also the state change from unlearned to learned was used by  Sloboda and Chechile (2008)  as the basis of a hazard function analysis of learning curves. Yet in other psychological applications, the critical event for a hazard analysis is the initiation of an eye movement (e.g.  Peterson, Kramer, Wang, Irwin, & McCarley, 2001; Yang & McConkie, 2001 ).

A number of theorems regarding hazard functions have been published in the psychological literature ( Chechile, 2003; Luce, 1986; Thomas, 1971; Townsend & Ashby, 1983 ). Many other useful results concerning hazard functions from a product reliability framework can be found in  Barlow and Proschan (1965, 1975) .

Part of the appeal of hazard function analysis is the potential for discriminating among theoretical proposals that are fairly similar. For example,  Chechile (2006)  considered more than a dozen functions that had been advanced in the psychological literature for describing the decline of memory retention with time. Empirical evidence was presented in that paper for the memory retention hazard function being peaked shaped, i.e., the hazard increased with retention interval to a peak and then decreased for longer retention intervals. Only four of sixteen proposed functions had this peaked shaped hazard property. Thus a hazard examination of memory retention provided powerful constraints on possible memory theories.  Luce (1986)  and  Van Zandt (2002)  have similarly observed qualitative differences in the hazard functions for some reaction time models with rather similar cumulative distributions. Consequently, hazard functions can be an important tool for evaluating competing psychological theories.

In stochastic modeling there also is a reverse hazard function  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      , which is defined as  
                         
                            (2) 
                            
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     F 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           d 
                                           F 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              x 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           d 
                                           x 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     F 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       In contrast to hazard, which represents the risk of a critical event occurring at  
                         x 
                       given that the event did not occur for lesser values of  
                         x 
                      , reverse hazard is the risk of a state change at  
                         x 
                       given that the event did occur either at  
                         x 
                       or for a lesser value. Of all the outcomes where the state change occurred,  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is the proportion of those outcomes that immediately occurred. A natural application of reverse hazard is for cases where the data are right-tail truncated. In fact this idea was initially employed in astronomy because the observational resolution of a telescope restricted the detection of objects, such as Quasars, to distances less than a specified value ( Lynden-Bell, 1971 ). The right tail corresponds to a region that is unobserved because it is beyond the optical resolution of the instrument. Thus at the limit of the observable range, the observed cumulative distribution is  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               o 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            U 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         1 
                      , but clearly this is incorrect because there might be many more Quasars in the unobservable right tail of the distribution. Consequently, the cumulative probability over the observable range is systematically off by an unknown amount, i.e., the true cumulative proportion at  
                         x 
                       is  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               t 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         c 
                         
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               o 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      , where  
                         c 
                       is an unknown constant. The consequence of right-tail truncation causes the observed hazard,  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               o 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      , to be different than the true hazard,

                               c

                                        )

                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     o

                      . In contradistinction to hazard, reverse hazard is not altered by right-tail truncation, i.e.,

Woodroofe (1985)  first used the term reverse hazard for this mathematical construction to deal with other cases of right-tail truncated data. The term retrohazard has also been used by other statisticians to describe  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       ( Gross & Huber-Carol, 1992 ). Researchers in the area of reliability engineering have found reverse hazard functions to be valuable ( Block, Savits, & Singh, 1998; Chandra & Roy, 2001; Finkelstein, 2002 ). In psychology,  Townsend and Wenger (2004)  have found  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       and integrated reverse hazard to be useful in their modeling of information processing capacity.

As with hazard functions, there are cases where two probability models can look very similar in terms of the cumulative probability but show qualitative differences in terms of reverse hazard. For example, consider the application of hazard theory to the area of decision making under conditions of uncertainty. A prototype for utility models is a lottery or gamble; the most simple case being where there are only two possible outcomes. Let

                            )

                         p 
                       until there is a valley and then increase for large  
                         p 
                       values. However, the reverse hazard for these models are qualitatively different, as is apparent in  Fig. 2 
                      . For the power model the reverse hazard decreases over the whole domain of support; whereas for the Prelec model the reverse hazard decreases for smaller values for  
                         p 
                       but increases for larger  
                         p 
                       values. The reverse hazard function thus provides a means for assessing some of the proposals for the risky weighting functions in utility theory.

The above example underscores the potential import of reverse hazard for revealing features of a stochastic theory, and it provides a means for testing theories. Although less attention has been devoted to reverse hazard heretofore, reverse hazard provides another way for understanding models.

There are a number of parallels between hazard and reverse hazard. Both functions are nonnegative values, and both functions can be applied to continuous random variables as well as to discrete probability distributions. Both functions are a general alternative framework for examining probability distributions. For example, given either a hazard function or a reverse hazard function, there is a corresponding cumulative distribution and a density function. Also starting with either discrete hazard or with discrete reverse hazard, the original discrete probability distribution can be recovered. Both hazard and reverse hazard can also be statistically challenging over some parts of the domain of support. Hazard estimates in the extreme right tail of the distribution are problematic because of statistical bias and because the denominator of  (1)  is approaching zero ( Aalen, 1978; Klein & Moeschberger, 1997; Nelson, 1972; Van Zandt, 2000, 2002 ). Note that in this region the reverse hazard is well behaved since  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       approaches  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       because the denominator of  (2)  approaches one. However, empirical estimates of  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       are problematic in the extreme left tail for the same reason that hazard is unstable in the extreme right tail. Although methods have been advanced for nonparametric smoothing, empirical hazard and empirical reverse hazard values can have considerable error in portions of the domain of support ( Singpurwalla & Wong, 1983 ). Consequently, in this paper the focus is on mathematical analyses of theoretical functions in order to learn about hazard and reverse hazard. A case can be made that reverse hazard is the other half of a complete analysis of the conditional probability of change. Taken together, hazard and reverse hazard provide a different way to conceptualize stochastic models.

In Section  2  of this paper, a number of linking relationships between hazard and reverse hazard are provided for continuous random variables. Section  3  is concerned with the connection of hazard and reverse hazard to Fisher information and Shannon differential entropy. Section  4  deals with the integration of hazard and reverse hazard. In Section  5 , a number of results are developed for the analysis of reverse hazard; whereas mixtures of continuous distributions are dealt with in Section  6 . In Section  7 , results are provided for a system of  
                         n 
                       independent components that have various hazard properties. Section  8  is devoted to the application of reverse hazard to discrete probability distributions. Finally, in Section  9  some concluding observations are offered about the power of using both hazard and reverse hazard for understanding stochastic systems.

In many psychological applications of hazard analysis, the ordered dimension for support of the random variable is time  
                         t 
                         ≥ 
                         0 
                      . Yet in other applications, such as the discussion of risky weighting functions in  Figs. 1 and 2 , the support is on the probability domain, i.e., the  
                         
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ] 
                         
                       interval. For still other continuous probability distributions, such as the Gumbel distribution or the normal distribution, the support is on the set of real numbers. Consequently, in the subsequent general examination of continuous distributions, let us define the variable as  
                         x 
                       and define the domain of support as  
                         
                            [ 
                            L 
                            , 
                            U 
                            ] 
                         
                      .

                       The above theorems, especially ( 3 ) and ( 5 ), are remarkable in that they show how fundamental properties of any continuous probability distribution, such as the cumulative probability, the survivor function, the odds ratio  
                         ω 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       and the probability density are all simple functions of hazard and reverse hazard. Although these properties can also be described in terms of hazard and probability density, the use of just hazard and reverse hazard can capture these fundamental features of a continuous distribution. Reverse hazard is thus a complementary theoretical construct to hazard.

For any continuous probability distribution

The analysis of hazard functions is largely concerned with the rate of change of hazard. Based on the above concepts, two theorems can be proved that link the rate of change of one type of hazard to properties of the other hazard. One theorem deals with implication for hazard if the reverse hazard is nondecreasing over an interval, i.e., the effects on

                            ( 
                            x

Given that the exponential distribution does not have constant reverse hazard, then which distribution does have constant reverse hazard? Consider the mirror-reflected exponential distribution, i.e.,  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         k 
                         exp 
                         
                            ( 
                            k 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       where  
                         x 
                       is on the domain of support of  
                         
                            ( 
                            − 
                            ∞ 
                            , 
                            0 
                            ] 
                         
                      . 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         It might be tempting to call this distribution a negative exponential; however, the term negative exponential distribution has been used by many writers to refer to the standard exponential distribution. Consequently, the distribution is more aptly described as the mirror-reflected exponential distribution. 
                       Note that  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               ∫ 
                            
                            
                               − 
                               ∞ 
                            
                            
                               x 
                            
                         
                         k 
                         exp 
                         
                            ( 
                            k 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         
                         
                         d 
                         x 
                         = 
                         exp 
                         
                            ( 
                            k 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Hence,  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               k 
                               exp 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            
                               exp 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         
                         = 
                         k 
                      . Consequently, the mirror-reflected exponential distribution has constant reverse hazard, as noted previously by  Block et al. (1998) . Because this distribution has constant reverse hazard it belongs both to the set of nondecreasing reverse hazard distributions and to the set of nonincreasing reverse hazard distributions. Hence, from  Theorem 6  it follows that  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is increasing for the mirror-reflected exponential distribution, i.e.,  
                         
                            
                               d 
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               d 
                               x 
                            
                         
                         > 
                         0 
                      .

There are a number of probability distributions that are defined over the nonnegative reals. Examples of distributions where  
                         x 
                         ≥ 
                         0 
                       include the exponential, Weibull, Wald, chi-squared, F, and gamma distributions. For each of these cases it is possible to define a mirror-reflected distribution over the nonpositive reals in a fashion similar to the above case of considering the mirror-reflected exponential distribution. If  
                         x 
                       is the random variable of a distribution for nonnegative reals, then  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               m 
                               r 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         − 
                         x 
                       is the random variable for a mirror-reflected distribution over the nonpositive reals. To obtain the mirror-reflected density function, it is necessary to replace  
                         x 
                       with  
                         − 
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               m 
                               r 
                            
                         
                      . 
                         2 
                      
                      
                         2 
                         In the case of the gamma distribution, the mirror-reflective distribution has been called the Erlang distribution (see  Johnson, Kotz, & Balakrishnan, 1994 ). 
                       The next theorem links  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       between a distribution and its mirror-reflected counterpart distribution. This result has been noted previously ( Block et al., 1998; Finkelstein, 2002 ).

                                     r

                               0

                               x

                                        x

Finally, it should also be noted that the converse case for either  Theorem 6  or  Theorem 7  does not necessarily result in any general conclusions. Other techniques of analysis are needed when either

The Fisher information measure is a valuable construct that has a number of important applications. It is useful in Bayesian analyses ( Jefreys, 1961 ) as well as in the determination of the Cramér–Rao inequality ( Stuart, Ord, & Arnold, 1999 ) and in the minimum description length statistic ( Grünwald, 2005 ). In the case where the probability model has a single parameter  
                         θ 
                       in the likelihood function and with standard regularity conditions ( Lehmann & Casella, 1998 ), the Fisher information measure

Efron and Johnstone (1990)  noted that the Fisher information matrix can also be computed by exchanging the hazard function for the density. This remarkable insight was not proved, but they stated that it could be easily shown.  , Gupta, Gupta, and Sankaran (2004)  further showed the connection between Fisher information and reverse hazard. These remarkable results are formally presented in the next theorem. The  Gupta et al. (2004)  proof is rather lengthy, but a new straightforward proof for both the hazard and reverse hazard connection to Fisher information is provided in the  Appendix .

Because there are a number of methods for computing the Fisher information matrix, there can be a choice of method for the calculation. As an example, let us compute the Fisher information for the exponential distribution, viz.,  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         θ 
                         
                            
                               e 
                            
                            
                               − 
                               θ 
                               x 
                            
                         
                       for  
                         x 
                         ≥ 
                         0 
                      . Because  
                         m 
                         = 
                         1 
                      , the Fisher information matrix has a single term that is denoted as  
                         I 
                         
                            ( 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                         
                      . It is well known that the hazard for the exponential distribution is a constant, i.e.,  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         θ 
                      . Hence,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  [ 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∂ 
                                        ln 
                                        λ

                                           x 
                                           , 
                                           θ 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ∂ 
                                        θ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ] 
                               
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               1 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     θ 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      . The expected value of a constant is the constant itself, so  
                         I 
                         
                            ( 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               1 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     θ 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                       for the exponential distribution. The Fisher information for the exponential distribution is more effortful to compute for either the  
                         E

Shannon differential entropy is an extension of Shannon uncertainty from a discrete distribution to a continuous probability distribution. The Shannon differential entropy for a continuous univariate distribution is defined as  
                         
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     Θ 
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               − 
                               E 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  ln 
                                  f 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        Θ 
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ] 
                               
                               = 
                               − 
                               
                                  
                                     ∫ 
                                  
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     U 
                                  
                               
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     Θ 
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               ln 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  f 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        Θ 
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ] 
                               
                               
                               d 
                               x 
                               . 
                            
                         
                       Note for Shannon uncertainty for discrete sequences, the base of the logarithm is 2; however, for differential entropy, the base of the logarithm is  
                         e 
                       because it is proportional to the base-two logarithm and because the base- 
                         e 
                       logarithm is more convenient for most statistical applications.

Given the linkage between Fisher information with hazard and reverse hazard, it is reasonable to suspect that Shannon differential entropy also has a connection with

                            x

                            x

It is straightforward to link hazard with the cumulative probability and density. Many investigators have found this connection to be useful (see  Chechile, 2003; Gupta & Panchapakesan, 1974; Kalbfleisch & Prentice, 2002; Lee, 1992; Townsend & Ashby, 1983 ). The linking formula comes from the integration of hazard over the  
                         
                            [ 
                            L 
                            , 
                            x 
                            ] 
                         
                       interval, i.e.,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ∫ 
                                  
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               
                               λ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  y 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                               d 
                               y 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     ∫ 
                                  
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     
                                     d 
                                     y 
                                  
                                  
                                     S 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               = 
                               − 
                               
                                  
                                     ∫ 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                                  
                                     S 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     d 
                                     S 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     S 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               = 
                               − 
                               log 
                               S 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x

                               . 
                            
                         
                       It thus follows that  
                         
                            (11) 
                            
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x

                               1 
                               − 
                               exp 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  −

                       Note that when  
                         x 
                         =

                      ,  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         0 
                      . Also for a normalized probability distribution the cumulative probability converges to 1 at  
                         x

                      , i.e.,  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            U

                         = 
                         1 
                      . Hence from  (11)  it is necessary that the integrated hazard diverges to infinity as  
                         x 
                         →

                       for probability distributions.

However, it is also possible to have a stochastic model where the hazard does not diverge as  
                         x 
                         → 
                         U 
                      . Let

                               U 
                            
                         
                       denote the integrated hazard over the domain of support. Note if

                         log

                                  1

                                  − 
                                  b 
                               
                            
                            )

                         >

                         > 
                         0 
                      , then  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            U 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         b 
                      . In probability theory, these models are called subprobability models ( Bertoin & LeJan, 1992; Graczyk, 1994; Haydn, Lacroix, & Vaienti, 2005 ). Subprobabilities are mathematical measures like probabilities, but they do not sum to 1 over the domain of support.

In psychology,  Chechile (2006)  used subprobabilities for an application of hazard functions to memory retention. For memory retention the domain of support is finite, i.e., the lifetime of the individual. It is possible that the critical event of a loss of a memory trace might not occur during the individual’s life. Subprobability hazard models are one way to capture this reality. With the two-trace hazard theory ( Chechile, 2006 ), it is hypothesized that all encoded events have initially two traces. One trace is assumed to have increasing hazard, and the integrated hazard from this trace diverges, i.e., the trace is based on a standard Weibull probability model with a shape parameter  
                         c 
                         = 
                         2 
                      . However, the second trace has monotonically decreasing hazard and is based on a subprobability model.  Chechile (2006)  used a Weibull probability model with a shape parameter  
                         1 
                         > 
                         c 
                         > 
                         0 
                       and multiplied the density by a constant  
                         b 
                       where  
                         0 
                         < 
                         b 
                         < 
                         1 
                      . 
                         3 
                      
                      
                         3 
                         It is well known and shown again in  Chechile (2003)  that the standard Weibull probability distribution has increasing hazard when the shape parameter  
                               c 
                             is greater than 1, and has constant hazard when  
                               c 
                               = 
                               1 
                             and has monotonically decreasing hazard when  
                               1 
                               > 
                               c 
                               > 
                               0 
                            . Even in the case when  
                               c 
                               < 
                               1 
                            , the integrated hazard for the Weibull distribution diverges to infinity. It is the multiplying the Weibull density by a constant  
                               b 
                             where  
                               1 
                               > 
                               b 
                               > 
                               0 
                             that converts the Weibull probability distribution into a subprobability distribution. 
                       This multiplication of the Weibull density for the second trace by  
                         b 
                       causes the probability distribution for the second trace to become a subprobability, i.e.,  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            U 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         b 
                      . In general, subprobability models can also be easily produced from any regular probability distribution by multiplying the density by a constant  
                         b 
                       where  
                         0 
                         < 
                         b 
                         < 
                         1 
                      . 
                         4 
                      
                      
                         4 
                         Some theorists might choose to use a mixture model instead of a subprobability model to capture the fact that not all memories are lost. A mixture model would hypothesize that  
                               1 
                               − 
                               b 
                             proportion of the memory traces are immune from storage loss over the life of the individual, and this immunity is in place even at the time of the initial memory encoding; thus only  
                               b 
                             proportion of the items are eventually lost. However,  Chechile (2006)  argued that there was not empirical evidence to support the assumption that some memories were initially immune from storage loss. Thus,  Chechile (2006)  preferred to use a subprobability model instead of a probability mixture, despite that fact that the resulting density function for the two approaches are the same.

Another example of a subprobability distribution in the psychological literature comes from the  Townsend (1976)  study of conditions for the equivalence in the minimum completion time between serial and parallel models.  Townsend (1976)  discovered a case where there was nonequivalence of the two cognitive architectures because one of the systems resulted in a subprobability model whereas the other did not. Although  Townsend (1976)  did not use the term subprobability, it was nonetheless a early example of the use of a subprobability distribution in psychological theory.

Subprobability theory is similar to standard probability theory. Theorems from probability theory are also true in subprobability theory, provided that the theorem does not depend on the sum of all the subprobabilities being 1. Yet it is easy to violate this constraint, so researchers should be very careful working with subprobability distributions. For example, from the previous section  Theorems 1–3 and 5 , and  Corollaries 1 and 2  are not true for subprobability distributions. In each case the proof of the theorem or corollary does not proceed unless the  
                         b 
                       value for the subprobability distribution is 1. However,  Theorems 4 and 6–8  are also true for subprobability distributions because each statement in the proofs for these theorems do not depend on the sum of the probabilities being 1.

Although the fully integrated hazard is finite for subprobability models and diverges for probability models, the integrated reverse hazard over the entire domain of support always diverges. Nonetheless,  Theorem 11  is still important because many distributions do have finite lower limits, and also the theorem places a strong constraint on the rate of change for the reverse hazard. This last point is the focus of the next theorem.

Theorem 12 
                         
                            Reverse hazard for continuous distributions cannot be monotonically increasing. 
                         
                      
                      Block et al. (1998)  stated without proof that reverse hazard cannot be increasing over the full interval of support. Yet the rationale for that result is not obvious, so a proof of the theorem is important, and can be found in the  Appendix .

Reverse hazard for continuous distributions cannot be monotonically increasing.

In light of  Theorem 12  the reverse hazard can be monotonically decreasing or have peaks or valleys where

                         > 
                         v 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       indicating that  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is decreasing initially for  
                         x 
                         ≤ 
                         . 
                         5 
                      . For  
                         x 
                         > 
                         . 
                         5 
                      , Mathematica plots were used to examine the behavior of  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         v 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      . In fact  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            )

                         v 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       for  
                         x

                            [ 
                            . 
                            5 
                            , 
                            . 
                            7210603 
                            ) 
                         
                       interval; thus

                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is still decreasing. However,  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         v 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       for  
                         x 
                         =

                         7210603 
                      . The ratio  
                         
                            
                               v 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  x 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     tan 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                              − 
                                              x

                                           x 
                                           )

                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                         > 
                         1 
                       for  
                         x 
                         > 
                         . 
                         7210603 
                      . Consequently, the reverse hazard decreases initially for this distribution to a valley at  
                         x 
                         = 
                         . 
                         7210603 
                       and increases for larger  
                         x 
                       values.

It should also be noted that  Theorem 13  is quite general and can apply both for continuous probability models as well as for subprobability models. This last point can be understood by observing that a probability model can be converted to a subprobability by multiplying the cumulative and density functions by  
                         b 
                       where  
                         0 
                         < 
                         b 
                         < 
                         1 
                      . This transformation, however, does not change the value for either  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       or  
                         v 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      , so  Theorem 13  holds independent of the value for  
                         b 
                      .

Another feature of  Theorem 13  is the focus of the next corollary.

Corollary 3 
                         
                            The reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing if the density function is monotonically decreasing.

The reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing if the density function is monotonically decreasing.

The exponential distribution  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         k 
                         
                            
                               e 
                            
                            
                               − 
                               k 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  − 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         
                       for  
                         x 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            [ 
                            L 
                            , 
                            ∞ 
                            ) 
                         
                       and the beta distribution with coefficients  
                         α 
                         = 
                         1 
                       and  
                         β 
                         > 
                         1 
                      , i.e.,  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         β 
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  1 
                                  − 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            
                               β 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                         
                       for  
                         x 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ] 
                         
                      , are two examples of distributions with monotonically decreasing density. It follows from  Corollary 3  that these distributions have monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

Theorem 13  is a general method for studying the behavior of the reverse hazard function; however, there are stochastic models where there is not a closed form formula for  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      , and it follows that there is not a closed form equation for the reverse hazard function. The normal distribution is a noteworthy example of such a case without a known closed form equation for either  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       or  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      . The next theorem provides a method for assessing the reverse hazard function without using the cumulative function. However, before directly establishing the theorem, it is convenient to prove the following lemma.

                            ( 
                            x

                         f 
                         
                            (

                            ( 
                            x

The normal distribution and beta distribution are two continuous models that do not have a known closed form function for the cumulative probability; however, with  Theorem 14  it is possible to assess the reverse hazard for these distributions. The next two corollaries deal with these distributions.

Corollary 4 
                         
                            The normal distribution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

The normal distribution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

                            The beta distribution with density  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               =

It should be pointed out that  Theorem 14  establishes that  
                         
                            
                               d 
                               v 
                            
                            
                               d 
                               x 
                            
                         
                         < 
                         0 
                         ⟹ 
                         
                            
                               d 
                               r 
                            
                            
                               d 
                               x 
                            
                         
                         < 
                         0 
                       and is a powerful tool for examining reverse hazard. However, there is no claim in the theorem about  
                         
                            
                               d 
                               r 
                            
                            
                               d 
                               x 
                            
                         
                         < 
                         0 
                         ⟹ 
                         
                            
                               d 
                               v 
                            
                            
                               d 
                               x 
                            
                         
                         < 
                         0 
                      . But it is possible to develop another set of powerful theorems by linking  
                         
                            
                               d 
                               r 
                            
                            
                               d 
                               x 
                            
                         
                       with theorems used for examining hazard  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      . However, it is first necessary to reestablish a counterpart theorem to  Theorem 14  that connects the strict monotonicity of  
                         g 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       with the monotonicity of  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Following a proof by  Thomas (1971) ,  Chechile (2003)  showed that a monotonicity for  
                         g 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       implied a monotonicity for  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      .  Chechile (2003)  also claimed (without proof) the converse that monotonicity in  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       implied a monotonic pattern for  
                         g 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      . The original proof also dealt incorrectly with peaks and valleys in the hazard function, but those flaws were corrected in  Chechile (2009) . But the correction still did not provide a proof that linked monotonicity in  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       with monotonicity in  
                         g 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Given these shortcomings in the previous proofs of this important theorem, the core ideas are reformulated with the missing points added.

                            For a continuous probability distribution with

Theorem 15  is of considerable importance for reverse hazard as will be clarified by the next theorem.

Theorem 16 
                         
                            For a continuous probability distribution with  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               F

                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                            , the reverse hazard  
                            
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is monotonically decreasing if the hazard  
                            
                               λ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is monotonically increasing.

For a continuous probability distribution with  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                            , the reverse hazard  
                            
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is monotonically decreasing if the hazard  
                            
                               λ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is monotonically increasing.

Theorem 7  established for continuous probability distributions that there is monotonically decreasing reverse hazard if there is monotonically decreasing (or at least nonincreasing) hazard. Now  Theorem 16  shows that monotonically decreasing reverse hazard also results when a continuous probability distribution with  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            U 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         0 
                       has monotonically increasing hazard. Although there are many cases covered by these two theorems, there are other stochastic models that are not covered. Examples of cases not covered by these theorems include: (a) probability models where  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is increasing but where  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            U 
                            ) 
                         
                         ≠ 
                         0 
                      , (b) models where the condition  
                         
                            
                               d 
                               v 
                            
                            
                               d 
                               x 
                            
                         
                         < 
                         0 
                       for all  
                         x 
                       does not hold, and (c) subprobability models. In fact several of these situations occurred in two examples where there is a reverse hazard function that initially decreases to a valley and increases for larger  
                         x 
                       values, i.e., the Prelec function in  Fig. 1  and the Jeffreys prior for the binomial. Of course very general results, such as  Theorem 13 , still can be used to learn about reverse hazard without limiting conditions.
