The next theorem deals with the product of two more functions which are individually PF 2  functions.

Theorem 18 
                         
                            For a continuous probability distribution with  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                            , the reverse hazard  
                            
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is monotonically decreasing if the density  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a product of two or more  PF 2   functions.

For a continuous probability distribution with  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                            , the reverse hazard  
                            
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is monotonically decreasing if the density  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a product of two or more  PF 2   functions.

Theorem 18  can be useful for psychological models where the overall random variable is composed of a product of the random variables that individually have increasing hazard. The supposition of increasing hazard for the component random variables is not unusual since the normal distribution has increasing hazard. As a potential example of a process that is represented by a product of random variables, consider the overall psychological well being for an individual. An additive model for component dimensions of utility may miss the point that the individual’s well being would be impaired if any one of the component dimensions is low. A product relationship better represents this type of psychological construct than an additive utility model. For such an application,  Theorem 18  could prove to be a valuable tool.

There has already been considerable interest in mathematical psychology in the sum of random variables, i.e., the convolution operation where  
                         
                            (16) 
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     ∫ 
                                  
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  s 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  − 
                                  s 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                               d 
                               s 
                               . 
                            
                         
                       The ex-Gaussian distribution results from the convolution of an exponential distribution with a Gaussian distribution. The ex-Gaussian distribution has been used extensively as a model for response times ( Hockley, 1984; Hohle, 1965; Ratcliff, 1978; Ratcliff & Murdock, 1976; Smith & Mewhort, 1998 ). Moreover,  Ashby and Townsend (1980)  have used the Donders (1868/1969) ( Donders, 1969 ) pure insertion method on a distributional level, and this operation results in the probability distribution for completion times as a convolution of random variables. The following theorem deals with the convolution of random variables.

Theorem 19 
                         
                            Given two random variables each with continuous probability distributions with  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             
                            and both have nondecreasing hazard, the convolution of these variables has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

Given two random variables each with continuous probability distributions with  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             
                            and both have nondecreasing hazard, the convolution of these variables has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

It is well known that the exponential distribution has constant hazard (which is nondecreasing hazard), and the Gaussian distribution has increasing hazard (which is also nondecreasing). Thus it is clear from  Theorem 19  that the ex-Gaussian distribution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

Barlow, Marshall, and Proschan (1963)  showed that the convolution of some specific random variables, which individually have decreasing hazard, results in a monotonically increasing hazard rate for the convolution. While these examples illustrate the possibility that the convolution of random variables with decreasing hazard can result in an increasing hazard rate for the convolution, there is no general proof that all such convolutions will have an increasing hazard rate. However, a general proof can be achieved for the reverse hazard of a convolution of variables with decreasing hazard.  Shaked and Shanthikumar (1994)  stated without proof that the convolution of two variables with decreasing reverse hazard has decreasing reverse hazard. This result is restated below and proved in the  Appendix .

Theorem 20 
                         
                            Given two continuous random variables that individually have monotonically decreasing hazard, the convolution of these variables has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

Given two continuous random variables that individually have monotonically decreasing hazard, the convolution of these variables has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

Theorems 19 and 20  form a powerful set of constraints on the reverse hazard rate of a convolution of random variables. It might be possible that there are distributions that combine via a convolution operation that results in a reverse hazard rate that is not a monotonically decreasing function. However, the component distributions for such a case cannot have either nondecreasing hazard with  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            U 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         0 
                       or monotonically decreasing hazard.

Mixture models have received considerable attention in mathematical psychology. In fact one way to understand multinomial processing tree models is as a mixture model of latent components ( Batchelder & Riefer, 1999; Chechile, 2006 ). A number of researchers have pointed out the importance of mixtures for the analysis of response time data (e.g.  Burbeck & Luce, 1982; Van Zandt & Ratcliff, 1995; Yantis, Meyer, & Smith, 1991 ).  Chechile (2003)  showed that mixtures can alter the hazard from that of the component distributions.  Sengupta and Nanda (1999)  as well as  Li, Da, and Zhao (2010)  have examined the reverse hazard of mixtures.

In general a mixture can be represented as

Theorem 21 
                         
                            The reverse hazard function is monotonically decreasing for a mixture of continuous component distributions that have nonincreasing hazard.

The reverse hazard function is monotonically decreasing for a mixture of continuous component distributions that have nonincreasing hazard.

Although a mixture of nonincreasing hazard components results in a monotonically decreasing hazard rate and a decreasing reverse hazard rate, the mixture of components that individually have increasing hazard is more complex.  Chechile (2003)  showed for the two-component case that the hazard profile was not monotonically increasing if the components are spaced sufficiently apart. In this case, the hazard increased to a peak then decreased to a valley and increased again for larger  
                         x 
                       values. This type of hazard profile was described as a PVI profile for a “Peak”, “Valley”, and “Increasing” hazard shape. However, if the two components of the mixture have component means that are separated less than a critical value, then the hazard profile for the mixture is monotonically increasing. This shift from monotonically increasing hazard with sufficient spacing among the components also occurs for three-component mixtures; in this case the hazard profile has a PVPVI structure.

In light of these findings, the question arises as to the profile of reverse hazard for mixtures that have components that individually have monotonically decreasing reverse hazard. To explore this question, a 50%–50% mixture of logistic distributions was examined. It was previously established that the logistic distributions have monotonically decreasing reverse hazard and monotonically increasing hazard. See  Fig. 4 
                       for a plot of the hazard  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       and the reverse hazard  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       for the case when the two component means are respectively, .0 and 4.5. The hazard function (solid line) exhibits the PVI profile that is typical of a mixture of monotonically increasing hazard components that are sufficiently separated. The reverse hazard plot (dashed line) is a symmetric function relative to the hazard function. The reverse hazard decreases to a valley, increases to a peak, and decreases again for larger  
                         x 
                       values, i.e., a DVPD profile. Moreover, if the separation between the components is kept at 4.5 and the mixture rate is altered, then the symmetry between  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       vanishes. When the component located at 4.5 has a greater weight in the mixture, then the hazard profile is less pronounced in showing qualitative changes but the reverse hazard still exhibits a sharply defined valley and peak. However, when the component located at 4.5 has reduced weight, then the hazard function exhibits a sharp peak and valley but the reverse hazard function has a less pronounced valley and peak. For the case of a 50%–50% mixture the peaks and valleys are flattened when the separation between the components is reduced to be in the range of 3.8–4.0. For smaller separations between the components, the two functions appear to be monotonic.

Another consideration regarding the reverse hazard properties of complex systems is the basis by which a multi-component system fails. One possible arrangement is where there is redundancy and “failure” occurs only when all the components fail. The  Hintzman (1986, 1988)  multiple-trace model for memory is an example of this type of cognitive architecture. Another example comes from the area of response time models where all the component tasks must be completed before a response. This type of task is called an AND task, and it can be implemented with either a serial exhaustive processing system or an exhaustive parallel architecture ( Townsend & Wenger, 2004 ). Yet another example comes from the  Chechile (2006)  two-trace hazard theory where memory storage failure only occurs when two separate memory traces are lost.  Chechile (2003)  called systems of this structure, conjunctive hazard systems, because failure only occurs with the conjunction of the failure of all of the separate memory copies.

Different than a conjunctive system, another arrangement of the components can result in a state change (like memory failure or task completion) because a single component changes state. These systems have no redundancy or backups, so the system requires the survival of all of the component parts.  Chechile (2003)  called such systems disjunctive hazard systems because the system transition occurs when any of the components transitions. For example, race models for response time can be represented as a system of separate parallel components that race to complete their task, and a response decision occurs when the fastest component finishes (see  Egeth, Folk, & Mullin, 1989; Mordkoff & Yantis, 1991 ). In terms of cognitive architectures, it is also possible for a disjunctive system to be any minimum time or self-terminating processing model, such as a parallel, serial or a hybrid mixture model (see  Townsend & Wenger, 2004 ). 
                         6 
                      
                      
                         6 
                         Researchers interested in discriminating among cognitive architectures for response times with either disjunctive tasks or conjunctive tasks have used a number of tools in addition to hazard functions and reverse hazard functions. These tools include the capacity coefficient ratios of integrated hazards ( Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ) and integrated reverse hazard functions ( Townsend & Wenger, 2004 ), ratios of hazard functions ( Wenger & Townsend, 2000 ), and the Cox proportional hazard functions ( Wenger & Gibbson, 2004 ). However, in this paper the focus is on reverse hazard functions. 
                       Some of the key mathematical results for disjunctive and conjunctive systems, in terms of hazard  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      , can be found in  Bain (1978)  and  Chechile (2003) . Here the focus is on the reverse hazard properties for conjunctive and disjunctive hazard systems.

Both hazard and reverse hazard also can be employed for discrete probability distributions. However, it is not always meaningful to use a hazard representation for discrete probability distributions. In order to use a hazard (or reverse hazard) framework there needs to be an underlying ordered dimension over which there are discrete probabilities. For example, the multinomial categories for a cognitive task have discrete probabilities for the various categorical outcomes, but there might not be a natural ordering of these categories. Such discrete distributions are not candidates for a hazard analysis. Yet there are other cases where there is an underlying ordered dimension. For example both the binomial distribution and Poisson distribution are distributed over nonnegative integers. As another example, consider the multinomial categories associated with a six-point rating scale used in a signal detection task. The subjects are typically instructed to use a higher rating if they are certain that a signal is present (see  Green & Swets, 1966 , p. 42). In a similar fashion, memory researchers might use a combination of a yes/no response along with a three-point confidence ratings to obtain an ordered arrangement of response categories, i.e., no-3, no-2, no-1, yes-1, yes-2, and yes-3 where higher ratings correspond to a more confident response (see  Chechile & Meyer, 1976; Macmillan & Creelman, 2005 ). It is applications such as these that allow for a hazard and reverse hazard analysis of discrete distributions.

Given an ordered set of discrete probabilities

                          Moreover, based on  (23)  a tree representation can be used for discrete distributions. Consequently, the entire discrete distribution can be conceptualized in terms of a hazard tree like the one shown in  Fig. 5 
                          (taken from  Chechile, 2003 ) where all the branch probabilities are functions of hazard values. In light of this useful conceptualization of the discrete distribution in terms of hazard, it is important to see if there is a similar representation in terms of reverse hazard. The next theorem is the basis of a reverse hazard tree.

Based on  Theorem 24  it is possible to represent a general discrete probability distribution as a reverse hazard tree. See such a representation in  Fig. 6 
                         . Note that the root of the reverse hazard tree is the  
                            M 
                            
                               th

                            k

                            1

                            k 
                            = 
                            1 
                          node. However, in terms of the reverse hazard tree, the computation begins with the  
                            M 
                         th node.

There are a number of implications of the fact that  
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            1 
                          for all discrete distributions. The next theorem deals with the consequence of reverse hazard being 1 for the lower limit of the discrete distribution.

Theorem 27 
                            
                               For a discrete random variable, the reverse hazard cannot be either monotonically increasing or a constant.

For a discrete random variable, the reverse hazard cannot be either monotonically increasing or a constant.

Theorem 27  highlights some differences between discrete reverse hazard and continuous reverse hazard. Namely, it was possible to have a continuous distribution with constant reverse hazard; that result occurred because the lower limit of the domain of support for the continuous variable was  
                            − 
                            ∞ 
                         . The divergence of reverse hazard did not occur for domains where  
                            L 
                            = 
                            − 
                            ∞ 
                          (see  Theorem 11 ). Consequently, the mirror-reflected exponential has constant continuous reverse hazard, i.e., the distribution  
                            f 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            k 
                            exp 
                            
                               ( 
                               k 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                          where  
                            x 
                          is in the domain of support of  
                            
                               ( 
                               − 
                               ∞ 
                               , 
                               0 
                               ] 
                            
                         . However, the support of a discrete distribution is restricted to positive integers, i.e.,  
                            k 
                            = 
                            1 
                          to  
                            M 
                         . Although  
                            M 
                          can be  
                            ∞ 
                         , it does not alter the fact that  
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          must be 1 even when there is an infinite set of discrete integers in the domain of support.

For discrete random variable, it is possible to have a distribution with constant hazard, i.e., the geometric distribution. The geometric distribution has

                                  1

                                  k 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            a 
                          for all  
                            k 
                         . But because  
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            1 
                         , there is no discrete distribution with constant reverse hazard for all  
                            k

                            k

There are several results that have a direct counterpart to results obtained for continuous distributions. For example, the next theorem is similar to  Theorem 7 .

Theorem 28 
                            
                               If the discrete hazard  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        λ 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               for a probability distribution is nonincreasing for all  
                               
                                  k 
                               
                               , then the discrete reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing.

                                        λ

                                  k 
                               
                               , then the discrete reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing.

The next theorem for discrete reverse hazard is a counterpart to  Corollary 3  developed for continuous distributions.

Theorem 29 
                            
                               The discrete reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing if  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                     
                                  
                                  > 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                        + 
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               for all  
                               
                                  k 
                               
                               .

                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                     
                                  
                                  > 
                                  
                                     
                                        p

An important discrete probability model in learning theory is the linear operator model (viz.  Coombs, Dawes, & Tversky, 1970 ). For this model, the incremental learning on trial  
                            k 
                            + 
                            1 
                          is a constant proportion of the remainder to be learned. If the asymptotic level of learning is 1, then  
                            
                               (30) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                        + 
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                     
                                  
                                  + 
                                  β 
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     1 
                                     − 
                                     
                                        
                                           F 
                                        
                                        
                                           k 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  0 
                                  < 
                                  β 
                                  < 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                               
                            
                          where  
                            β 
                          is the learning parameter. The next corollary deals with the reverse hazard for the linear operator learning model.

Corollary 7 
                            
                               Given no pre-experimental learning and a learning asymptote of 1, the linear operator learning model has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

Given no pre-experimental learning and a learning asymptote of 1, the linear operator learning model has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

An important tool for the analysis of continuous distributions is the examination of  
                            f 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                          to see if it belongs to the PF 2  class of functions.  Chechile (2003)  also employed PF 2  functions for the hazard analysis of discrete random variables. However, in the analysis of discrete reverse hazard it is more convenient to utilize the idea of “strong unimodality” developed by  Keilson and Gerber (1971) . By definition, a strong unimodal sequence or distribution has a mode, and the convolution of the distribution with another unimodal sequence also has a mode.  Keilson and Gerber (1971)  provided the necessary and sufficient condition for a strong unimodal sequence; namely it is any discrete distribution that satisfies the condition that  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ≥ 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  + 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          for all  
                            k 
                         . When the inequality is strict, then  Keilson and Gerber (1971)  further call the sequence “strictly log-concave” or a SLC sequence i.e.,  
                            
                               (31) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  > 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                        + 
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                        − 
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  .

Theorem 30 
                            
                               The reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing for a discrete distribution that satisfies   (31)   for  
                               
                                  k 
                                  = 
                                  2 
                                  , 
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  M 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                               
                               .

The reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing for a discrete distribution that satisfies   (31)   for  
                               
                                  k 
                                  = 
                                  2 
                                  , 
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  M 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                               
                               .

Theorem 30  is a powerful tool for examining a number of discrete distributions. The next two corollaries illustrates the utility of the theorem.

Corollary 8 
                            
                               The binomial distribution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

The binomial distribution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

Corollary 9 
                            
                               The Poisson distribution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

The Poisson distribution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

There are distributions that are strongly unimodal but are not SLC sequences. For example, the geometric distribution where  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            a 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     1 
                                     − 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          is strongly unimodal but it is not a SLC sequence because  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  + 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                         . In order for a sequence to be SLC, it is necessary that  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            > 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  + 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                         . Note that the geometric distribution, nonetheless, has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard. The proof of that assertion was based on  Theorem 28  and was not based on a demonstration of it being a SLC sequence. Consequently, the set of distributions that have monotonically decreasing reverse hazard is greater than the set of distributions that are SLC.

It is also noteworthy that  Theorem 30  applies for subprobability distributions as well as for probability distributions. The inductive proof for  Theorem 30  did not require the assumption that the probabilities added to 1, so a subprobability sequence has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard if it is a SLC sequence.

In order for the convolution of two discrete random variables to be meaningful there is a requirement that both distributions be on counts. The case of  
                            k 
                            = 
                            1 
                          corresponds to the count of 0 and in general the count ranges from 0 to  
                            M 
                            − 
                            1 
                         . The convolution of the two random variables is the sum of the counts.

Theorem 31 
                            
                               The convolution of a SLC sequence with either a strictly unimodal sequence or another SLC sequence results in a sequence that has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

The convolution of a SLC sequence with either a strictly unimodal sequence or another SLC sequence results in a sequence that has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

Previously it was shown that the geometric distribution was a strictly unimodal sequence but was not a SLC sequence.  Chechile (2003)  showed that the convolution of two geometric distributions resulted in a distribution with monotonically increasing hazard. The next theorem examines the convolution of two geometric distributions in terms of reverse hazard.

Theorem 32 
                            
                               The convolution of two geometric distributions has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

The convolution of two geometric distributions has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.

There is a parallel between the hazard analysis of continuous mixtures and the analysis of mixtures for discrete random variables. The next theorem is the counterpart to  Theorem 21  that dealt with continuous mixtures.

Theorem 33 
                            
                               The reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing for a mixture of discrete distributions that individually have nonincreasing hazard.

The reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing for a mixture of discrete distributions that individually have nonincreasing hazard.

The reverse hazard is more complex for the case where there is a mixture of discrete distributions that individually have increasing hazard. In general the same features occur with discrete mixture as discussed previously in terms of continuous distributions. Sometimes the reverse hazard is still monotonically decreasing for the mixture, and on other occasions the reverse hazard decreases to a valley then increases to a peak and decreases again for larger  
                            k 
                          values.  Table 1 
                          illustrates the hazard and reverse hazard for two different mixtures. Mixture  
                            A 
                          has one component that is a binomial with parameter  
                            ϕ 
                            = 
                            . 
                            2 
                         ; whereas the other component is a binomial with a parameter of  
                            ϕ 
                            = 
                            . 
                            8 
                         . The lower component is given a weight of 99.9% and the other component has a weight of .1%. There are  
                            n 
                            = 
                            10 
                          trials, so there are  
                            M 
                            = 
                            11 
                          outcomes. Note that the  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          values do not exhibit any evidence of a multimodal distribution, i.e., there is a single mode at  
                            k 
                            = 
                            3 
                          corresponding to a count of 2. Also note that the  
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          distribution is monotonically decreasing for Mixture  
                            A 
                         , but  
                            
                               
                                  λ 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          values exhibit the typical PVI profile of a mixture. The other mixture illustrated in  Table 1  has the same two binomial components, but the weights for the components are switched, i.e., a .1% weight for the lower component and a 99.9% weight for the upper component. Again the distribution of  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          has only a single mode and does not exhibit evidence of a mixture. However, now the hazard  
                            
                               
                                  λ 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          is monotonically increasing, but the reverse hazard  
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          decreases to a valley, increases to a peak and decreases again for larger  
                            k 
                          values. Due to the symmetry between these two mixtures  
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  mixture A 
                               
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  λ 
                               
                               
                                  M 
                                  + 
                                  1 
                                  − 
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  mixture B 
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  λ 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  mixture A 
                               
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  M 
                                  + 
                                  1 
                                  − 
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  mixture B 
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         . This example illustrates the analytic power of examining both hazard and reverse hazard. The two conditional probabilities ( 
                            
                               
                                  λ 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                         ) complement one another. When there is a tiny mixture component in the right tail, then hazard is effective in detecting that component; however, when there is a tiny mixture component in the left tail, then the reverse hazard is effective for detecting the mixture. In both cases the distributions failed to show any evidence of a mixture by being multimodal. The example illustrates that hazard and reverse hazard have the potential for being effective tools for detecting the presence of a mixture.

As with continuous random variables, there can be conjunctive and disjunctive hazard systems. For the conjunctive hazard system, the qualitative change in the state for the  
                            k 
                         th event only occurs when all of  
                            n 
                          independent components of the system have transitioned by the  
                            k 
                         th event. The analysis for the discrete conjunctive case was examined by  Chechile (2003) . Unfortunately, those results were awkward and incorrect. 
                            8 
                         
                         
                            8 
                            Theorem 17 from  Chechile (2003)  is incorrect because the quantity computed is not the probability of all components failing by the  
                                  k 
                               th event divided by the system survival function for the  
                                  k 
                               th event. 
                          However, the analysis of the reverse hazard for a conjunctive system is straightforward.

For a discrete disjunctive hazard system, the system fails when any single component of the system fails. There is a straightforward analysis of the hazard for disjunctive hazard systems of  
                            n 
                          independent components (see  Chechile, 2003 , Theorem 16). The counterpart for the reverse hazard analysis of disjunctive system is more complex. However, the hazard solution can be readily used to obtain the reverse hazard for a disjunctive system.

For continuous stochastic processes, reverse hazard cannot be monotonically increasing, but it can be monotonically decreasing or might have selective regions where the reverse hazard profile is either a constant or increasing, i.e., there might be a interval for  
                         x 
                       where  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is nondecreasing, and from  Theorem 6  we know that the hazard,  
                         λ 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      , is increasing for any region where  
                         r 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is nondecreasing. It is also possible for constant reverse hazard over the entire support interval, i.e., the mirror-reflected exponential distribution where  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         k 
                         
                         exp 
                         
                            ( 
                            k 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       for  
                         x 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            ( 
                            − 
                            ∞ 
                            , 
                            0 
                            ] 
                         
                      . When the reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing over the whole domain of support, there are many tests for detecting this reverse hazard property. In fact in  Table 2 
                       there are ten conditions identified that result in the conclusion that the reverse hazard is strictly decreasing. The methods in  Table 2  can be applied to show that many common distributions have monotonically decreasing reverse hazard. Some distributions with monotonically decreasing reverse hazard are shown in  Table 3 
                      . Also shown in  Table 3  is a method by which it can be proven that the reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing.

It is striking from  Table 2  how many conditions result in monotonically decreasing reverse hazard, although we have a seen a number of examples in this paper where the reverse hazard function is not monotonically decreasing. Moreover, it is straightforward to generate a new probability models with any particular hazard qualities desired, except for monotonically increasing reverse hazard. From  (12)  the cumulative probability of a new distribution is equal to an integral of the reverse hazard function, i.e.,

One of the valuable features of a hazard function approach for examining stochastic processes is the insight that can be gained beyond the information that is learned from just the cumulative probability or the density function. Hazard captures the dynamic changes in the probability of a state transition. Yet the analysis is enhanced by using both hazard and reverse hazard. These two functions offer complementary information and provide further constraints on the assessment of probabilistic systems. The example in  Table 1  illustrates that these two conditional probabilities are complementary constructs. Sometimes hazard might be the more revealing measure, and on other occasions reverse hazard might be the more valuable tool. Taken together, these two functions provide a new way for understanding basic constructs such as probability density and the odds ratio.

Finally it should be pointed out that hazard and reverse hazard can be informative even when the predicted form for both functions is roughly correct, but where there are still systematic discrepancies in the errors for hazard and reverse hazard. Consider the example provided in  Table 4 
                      . For this example there are ten intervals for the random variable  
                         x 
                      . Suppose that a researcher assumes that the discrete probability distribution for observations in these intervals,  
                         
                            
                               p 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         k 
                         = 
                         1 
                         , 
                         … 
                         , 
                         10 
                      , are based on an underlying continuous Weibull distribution with the shape parameter of 2, i.e., where  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               l 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               u 
                            
                         
                       are the respective lower and upper limits of the  
                         k 
                         
                            th 
                         
                       interval. However, suppose that the data actually originated from a Weibull distribution with a shape parameter of  
                         1.5 
                      , i.e.,  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               e 
                            
                            
                               − 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     l 
                                  
                                  
                                     1.5 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                         − 
                         
                            
                               e 
                            
                            
                               − 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     u 
                                  
                                  
                                     1.5 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      . Values for  
                         
                            
                               r 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            c 
                            t 
                            u 
                            a 
                            l 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            c 
                            t 
                            u 
                            a 
                            l 
                            ) 
                         
                       are shown in the table along with the researcher’s model-based values for  
                         
                            
                               r 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                      . Both hazard and reverse hazard have the predicted monotonicity pattern. Yet for intervals  
                         k 
                         = 
                         2 
                         , 
                         3 
                         , 
                         4 
                         , 
                         5 
                      , the theoretical predictions for reverse hazard are too large, and for intervals  
                         k 
                         = 
                         4 
                         , 
                         … 
                         , 
                         9 
                      , the theoretical predictions for  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                       are too large. There is a pattern to the errors in fitting reverse hazard and hazard. Such a pattern to the errors is a signal that the model might be misspecified. Thus the combination of hazard and reverse hazard provide for a more comprehensive method for understanding the dynamical properties of a stochastic system.

Proof for Theorem 12 
                         Consider two cases of either (1)  
                               L 
                             is finite or (2)  
                               L 
                               = 
                               − 
                               ∞ 
                            . For the first case,  Theorem 11  applies. Note that in the proof of  Theorem 11  it was necessary to stipulate in the case for finite  
                               L 
                             that the condition of  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0

                                        L

                                     d 
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             did not hold. Yet in the current theorem, the premise that the reverse hazard is increasing implies that

                               ∞

                               r

                                  x

                               ∞

                               x

                               F

                                  x

                               = 
                               0

                               x

Proof for Corollary 3 
                         If the density is monotonically decreasing, then  
                               
                                  
                                     d 
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     d 
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               < 
                               0 
                             for all  
                               x 
                             in the domain of support. Since  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is nonnegative, it follows that  
                               v 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               < 
                               0 
                               
                               ∀ 
                               
                               x 
                            . Hence, from  Theorem 13  it follows that  
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               > 
                               v 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            , so  
                               
                                  
                                     d 
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     d 
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               < 
                               0 
                            ; thus indicating that  
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is decreasing.  □

If the density is monotonically decreasing, then  
                               
                                  
                                     d 
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     d 
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               < 
                               0 
                             for all  
                               x 
                             in the domain of support. Since  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is nonnegative, it follows that  
                               v 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               < 
                               0 
                               
                               ∀ 
                               
                               x 
                            . Hence, from  Theorem 13  it follows that  
                               r 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               > 
                               v 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            , so  
                               
                                  
                                     d 
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     d 
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               < 
                               0 
                            ; thus indicating that  
                               r

                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is decreasing.  □

                             corresponding to a monotonically decreasing reverse hazard rate. From  Theorem 12  we know that it is not possible to have a monotonically increasing reverse hazard rate. Proof for Theorem 17 
                         
                            Chechile (2003)  showed that if  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  U 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             and  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is a PF 2  function, then  
                               λ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is either monotonically increasing for all  
                               x 
                             or  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is the exponential distribution. If  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is the exponential distribution, then the reverse hazard is decreasing because it is well known that the exponential distribution has constant hazard and thus has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard by means of  Theorem 7 . However, if  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             has increasing hazard then it follows from  Theorem 16  that the reverse hazard is monotonically decreasing.  □

                                  )

Proof of Theorem 18 
                         If the density is composed of the product of functions that are individually PF 2  functions, then the overall  
                               W 
                             from  (15)  is also composed of the product of  
                               W 
                             functions for the separate product components. Because the product of values that are greater than or equal to one must also be greater than or equal to one, it follows that  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             must be a PF 2  function. Hence by  Theorem 17  the reverse hazard must be monotonically decreasing. □

If the density is composed of the product of functions that are individually PF 2  functions, then the overall  
                               W 
                             from  (15)  is also composed of the product of  
                               W 
                             functions for the separate product components. Because the product of values that are greater than or equal to one must also be greater than or equal to one, it follows that  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                             must be a PF 2  function. Hence by  Theorem 17  the reverse hazard must be monotonically decreasing. □

Proof for Theorem 19 
                         
                            Barlow et al. (1963, pp. 36–37)  proved that the convolution of two independent continuous random variables has increasing hazard if the component distributions have either increasing hazard or at least nondecreasing hazard. Thus from  Theorem 16  it can be concluded that the convolution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.  □

Barlow et al. (1963, pp. 36–37)  proved that the convolution of two independent continuous random variables has increasing hazard if the component distributions have either increasing hazard or at least nondecreasing hazard. Thus from  Theorem 16  it can be concluded that the convolution has monotonically decreasing reverse hazard.  □

Proof for Theorem 21 
                         
                            Chechile (2003)  provided a lengthy, technical proof that the mixture hazard rate was monotonically decreasing if component probability distributions have either monotonically decreasing hazard or a constant hazard.  Chechile (2006)  generalized the proof to a mixture of subprobability distributions. Both proofs are inductive proofs. Given that the hazard is monotonically decreasing for the mixture, it follows from  Theorem 7  that the reverse hazard rate for the mixture is monotonically decreasing.  □

Chechile (2003)  provided a lengthy, technical proof that the mixture hazard rate was monotonically decreasing if component probability distributions have either monotonically decreasing hazard or a constant hazard.  Chechile (2006)  generalized the proof to a mixture of subprobability distributions. Both proofs are inductive proofs. Given that the hazard is monotonically decreasing for the mixture, it follows from  Theorem 7  that the reverse hazard rate for the mixture is monotonically decreasing.  □

Proof for Theorem 27 
                         Given that  
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            , it would have to be the case that  
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     k 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            , for all  
                               k 
                             in order to have constant reverse hazard. However, that would imply that  
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             for all  
                               i 
                               < 
                               k 
                             which contradicts the fact that the lower limit of the domain of support is 1 and not  
                               k 
                            . Consequently, there is no discrete distribution with constant hazard. The hypothesis of a monotonically increasing hazard is also absurd because for the case of  
                               k 
                               = 
                               2 
                             it would result in the erroneous conclusion that  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     + 
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               > 
                               1 
                            . Consequently, discrete reverse hazard cannot be monotonically increasing.  □
